items.title: Histogram grid for every numeric feature before training
items.scenario: A tabular ML project loads a pandas DataFrame with 12 numeric columns. The analyst calls `df.hist(figsize=(12, 8), bins=30)` or builds a `subplots` grid with `ax.hist` per column to spot skew, outliers, and zero-inflation before choosing scalers.
items.explanation: Matplotlib histograms are the first-line EDA tool for distribution shape—directly informing normalization and outlier handling decisions.

items.title: Training vs validation loss curves during model training
items.scenario: After each epoch, a PyTorch loop appends `train_loss` and `val_loss` to lists. At the end, `plt.plot(epochs, train_loss, label='train')` and `plt.plot(epochs, val_loss, label='val')` with a legend reveal overfitting when val loss diverges upward.
items.explanation: Line plots on shared axes with labeled series are the standard Matplotlib pattern for monitoring convergence—no special ML library required.

items.title: Scatter of two features colored by class label
items.scenario: On Iris, the practitioner plots sepal length vs width with a loop: for each species, `ax.scatter(subset_x, subset_y, label=species)`. The legend and axis labels make a notebook-ready EDA figure before trying classifiers.
items.explanation: Discrete label coloring via multiple scatter calls (or `c=y` with a colormap) is foundational; advanced embedding interpretation lives in the embedding visualization topic.

items.title: Bar chart of class imbalance in a classification dataset
items.scenario: Before training, `value_counts()` on the target column feeds `ax.bar(classes, counts)` with rotated x tick labels. The team decides whether to stratify splits or apply class weights based on the visible skew.
items.explanation: Bar plots communicate categorical frequency—common pre-modeling check linked to metric choice (see confusion matrix topic for evaluation views).
